sections.label: Section 1
sections.translation: Enter the Chorus of Trojan guards . Go to Hector ’ s couch . Which of you squires that tend the prince , or you armor-clad men , is awake ? He ought to receive fresh tidings

sections.label: Section 5
sections.translation: from the warriors who were set to guard the assembled army during the fourth watch of the night . Calls to Hector in the tent . Lift up your head ! Prop your arm beneath it ! Unseal that fierce eye from its repose ; quit your lowly couch of scattered leaves ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.translation: Hector ! It is time to hearken .

sections.label: Section 11
sections.original: τίς ὅδʼ ; ἦ φίλιος φθόγγος · τίς ἀνήρ ;
sections.translation: Who is this ? Is it a friend who calls ? Who are you ? Your password ? Speak ! Who are these who come near my couch in the night ? You must tell me .

sections.label: Section 15
sections.translation: Sentinels of the army .

sections.label: Section 15b
sections.translation: Why this tumultuous haste ?

sections.label: Section 16
sections.translation: Be of good courage .

sections.label: Section 16b
sections.translation: I am .

sections.label: Section 17
sections.translation: Is there some midnight ambush ?

sections.label: Section 17b
sections.translation: No .

sections.label: Section 17c
sections.translation: Why do you desert your post and rouse the army , unless you have some tidings of the night ?

sections.label: Section 20
sections.translation: Are you not aware how near the Argive army we take our night ’ s repose clad in all our armor ?

sections.label: Section 1.23
sections.translation: To arms ! Hector , seek your allies ’ sleeping camp !

sections.label: Section 1.25
sections.translation: Stir them up to wield the spear ! Awake them ! Despatch a friend to your own company , bridle the horses . Who will go to the son of Panthus ? Who to Europa ’ s son , captain of the Lycian band ?

sections.label: Section 1.30
sections.translation: Where are they who should inspect the victims ? Where are the leaders of the light-armed troops and the Phrygian archers ? String your horn-tipped bows !

sections.label: Section 34
sections.translation: Your tidings inspire now fear ,

sections.label: Section 35
sections.translation: now confidence ; nothing is plainly set forth . Can it be that you are smitten with wild fear by Pan , the son of Cronion ? Leaving your watch you rouse the army . What does your noisy summons mean ? What tidings can I say you bring ? Your words are many ,

sections.label: Section 40
sections.translation: but no plain statement have you made .

sections.label: Section 1.41
sections.translation: The long night through , Hector , the Argive army has kindled fires , and bright with torches shines the anchored fleet . To Agamemnon ’ s tent the whole army

sections.label: Section 1.45
sections.translation: move clamorously by night , eager for fresh orders ; for never before has the sea-faring company been so alarmed . And so I was suspicious of what might happen

sections.label: Section 1.50
sections.translation: and came to tell you , so that you may have no cause to blame me hereafter .

sections.label: Section 52
sections.original: ἐς καιρὸν ἥκεις , καίπερ ἀγγέλλων φόβον ·
sections.translation: In good season you come , although your tidings are full of terror ; for those men are bent on giving me the slip and stealing away from this land in their ships by night ;

sections.label: Section 55
sections.translation: their midnight signalling pleases me . Ah ! Fortune , to rob me in my hour of triumph , a lion of his prey , before this spear had made an end of the whole Argive army in one line ! Yes , if the sun ’ s bright lamp had not withheld

sections.label: Section 60
sections.translation: his light , I would not have stayed my victor ’ s spear , before I had fired their ships and made my way from tent to tent , drenching this hand in Achaean blood . I was eager to make a night attack and take advantage of the stroke of luck sent by heaven ;

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οἱ σοφοί με καὶ τὸ θεῖον εἰδότες
sections.translation: but those wise seers of mine , who have heaven ’ s will so pat , persuaded me to wait for dawn , and then leave not one Achaean in the land . But those others do not await the counsels of my soothsayers ; darkness turns runaways to heroes .

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὡς τάχιστα χρὴ παραγγέλλειν στρατῷ
sections.translation: But we must now without delay pass this word to the army to take up arms and cease from slumber , so that many an Achaean , as he leaps aboard his ship , shall be smitten through the back and sprinkle the ladders with blood , and others shall be fast bound with cords

sections.label: Section 75
sections.translation: and learn to till our Phrygian fields .

sections.label: Section 76
sections.original: Ἕκτορ , ταχύνεις πρὶν μαθεῖν τὸ δρώμενον ·
sections.translation: You hasten , Hector , before you know clearly what is happening ; for we do not know for certain whether the men are flying .

sections.label: Section 78
sections.original: τίς γὰρ πύρʼ αἴθειν πρόφασις Ἀργείων στρατόν ;
sections.translation: What other reason did the Argive army have to kindle fires ?

sections.label: Section 79
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ · ὕποπτον δʼ ἐστὶ κάρτʼ ἐμῇ φρενί .
sections.translation: I do not know ; I am very suspicious .

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: πάντʼ ἂν φοβηθεὶς ἴσθι , δειμαίνων τόδε .
sections.translation: If you fear this , be sure there ’ s nothing you would not fear .

sections.label: Section 81
sections.original: οὔπω πρὶν ἧψαν πολέμιοι τοσόνδε φῶς .
sections.translation: Never before did the enemy kindle such a blaze .

sections.label: Section 82
sections.original: οὐδʼ ὧδέ γʼ αἰσχρῶς ἔπεσον ἐν τροπῇ δορός .
sections.translation: No , nor ever before did they suffer such shameful defeat and rout .

sections.label: Section 83
sections.original: σὺ ταῦτʼ ἔπραξας · καὶ τὰ λοιπὰ νῦν σκόπει .
sections.translation: This you have achieved ; look now to what remains to do .

sections.label: Section 84
sections.original: ἁπλοῦς ἐπʼ ἐχθροῖς μῦθος ὁπλίζειν χέρα .
sections.translation: A simple tale , to arm against the enemy .

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὅδʼ Αἰνέας καὶ μάλα σπουδῇ ποδὸς
sections.translation: Look ! Here comes Aeneas , in great haste too , with news to tell his friends .

sections.label: Section 87
sections.original: Ἕκτορ , τί χρῆμα νύκτεροι κατὰ στρατὸν
sections.translation: Hector , why have the sentinels in terror made their way through the army at night to your couch to hold a midnight conclave and disturb the army ?

sections.label: Section 90
sections.translation: Encase yourself in your armor , Aeneas .

sections.label: Section 91
sections.original: τί δʼ ἔστι ; μῶν τις πολεμίων ἀγγέλλεται
sections.translation: What is it ? Have tidings come of some secret stratagem set on foot during the night by the foe ?

sections.label: Section 93
sections.translation: The men are flying , and going aboard their ships .

sections.label: Section 94
sections.original: τί τοῦδʼ ἂν εἴποις ἀσφαλὲς τεκμήριον ;
sections.translation: What sure proof can you give of this ?

sections.label: Section 95
sections.translation: The whole night they are kindling blazing torches ; I think they will not wait for tomorrow , but after lighting brands upon their ships ’ decks will leave this land and fly to their homes .

sections.label: Section 99
sections.original: σὺ δʼ ὡς τί δράσων πρὸς τάδʼ ὁπλίζῃ χέρας ;
sections.translation: And you , why do you gird on your sword ?

sections.label: Section 100
sections.translation: With my spear I will stop them even as they fly and leap aboard their ships , and my hand shall be heavy upon them ; for it is shameful in us , yes , and cowardly as well as shameful , when the god gives them into our hands , to let our foes escape without battle , after all the injuries they have done us .

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: εἴθʼ ἦσθʼ ἀνὴρ εὔβουλος ὡς δρᾶσαι χερί .
sections.translation: Would you were as sage as you are bold ! But , to be sure , among mortals the same man is not dowered by nature with universal knowledge ; each has his special gift appointed him , yours is battle , another ’ s is sage counsel . You are excited to hear that the Achaeans are

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: φλέγειν Ἀχαιούς , καὶ στρατὸν μέλλεις ἄγειν
sections.translation: lighting blazing torches , and you would lead on our troops across the ditches in the calm still night . Now after crossing the deep yawning trench , supposing you should find the enemy are not flying from the land , but are awaiting your onset ,

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: νικώμενος μὲν οὔτι μὴ μόλῃς πάλιν ·
sections.translation: beware lest you suffer defeat and so never reach this city again ; for how will you pass the palisades in a rout ? And how shall your charioteers cross the bridges without dashing the axles of their cars to pieces ? And , if victorious , you have next the son of Peleus to engage ;

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: ὅς σʼ οὐκ ἐάσει ναυσὶν ἐμβαλεῖν φλόγα ,
sections.translation: he will never allow you to cast the firebrand on the fleet or harry the Achaeans , as you believe . No , for that man is fierce as fire , a very tower of might . Let us rather then leave our men to sleep calmly under arms after the weariness of battle ,

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: κατάσκοπον δὲ πολεμίων , ὃς ἂν θέλῃ ,
sections.translation: while we send , as I advise , whoever will volunteer to spy upon the enemy ; and if they really are preparing to fly , let us arise and fall upon the Argive army , but if this signalling is a trap to catch us , we shall discover from the spy the enemy ’ s designs

sections.label: Section 130
sections.translation: and take counsel ; such is my advice , lord .

sections.label: Section 1.131
sections.translation: It pleases me well ; so change your mind and adopt this counsel . I do not love hazardous commands in generals . What is better than for a swift-footed spy to approach the ships

sections.label: Section 1.135
sections.original: πέλας ὅ τί ποτʼ ἄρα δαΐοις
sections.translation: and learn why our foes are lighting fires in front of their naval station ?

sections.label: Section 137
sections.translation: Since this finds favor with you all , prevail . To Aeneas . Go and calm the allies ; perhaps the army hearing of our midnight council is disturbed .

sections.label: Section 140
sections.translation: Mine shall it be to send one forth to spy upon the foe . And if I discover any plot among them , you shall fully hear of it and be present to learn the report ; but in case they are starting off in flight , with eager ear await the trumpet ’ s call ,

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: ὡς οὐ μενοῦντά μʼ · ἀλλὰ προσμείξω νεῶν
sections.translation: for then I will not stay , but will this very night engage the Argive army there where their ships are hauled up .

sections.label: Section 147
sections.original: πέμφʼ ὡς τάχιστα · νῦν γὰρ ἀσφαλῶς φρονεῖς .
sections.translation: Send out the spy at once ; there ’ s safety in your counsels now . And you shall find me steadfast at your side , whenever occasion calls .

sections.label: Section 149
sections.original: τίς δῆτα Τρώων οἳ πάρεισιν ἐν λόγῳ
sections.translation: What Trojan of those present in council

sections.label: Section 150
sections.original: θέλει κατόπτης ναῦς ἐπʼ Ἀργείων μολεῖν ;
sections.translation: volunteers to go and spy on the Argive fleet ? Who will be that patriot ? Who says yes ? I myself cannot at every point serve my country and my friends in arms .

sections.label: Section 154
sections.original: ἐγὼ πρὸ γαίας τόνδε κίνδυνον θέλω
sections.translation: I for my country will gladly run this risk

sections.label: Section 155
sections.original: ῥίψας κατόπτης ναῦς ἐπʼ Ἀργείων μολεῖν ,
sections.translation: and go to spy on the Argive fleet , and when I have learned fully all that the Achaeans plot I will return . I undertake this toil on these conditions .
